Panic disorder diagnosed 18 months ago after emergency department visit for 'panic attacks'
Mild iron-deficiency anemia, resolved with supplementation

medications.indication: Generalized anxiety disorder and panic disorder

medications.drug: Oral contraceptive (ethinyl estradiol 30 mcg / levonorgestrel 0.15 mg)

family_history: Maternal grandmother with 'spells' in her 40s, never formally diagnosed, reportedly stopped on their own
No known family history of epilepsy or seizure disorders

social_history.occupation: Graduate student in comparative literature, currently ABD on dissertation
social_history.recreational_drugs: Tried marijuana twice in college, no current use
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a studio apartment near university campus
social_history.sleep: Reports chronic sleep difficulties with 5-6 hours per night due to dissertation stress, frequent late-night reading
social_history.driving: Does not own a car, uses public transit and bicycle

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Affect appears mildly anxious but appropriate. Speech fluent, no word-finding difficulties, no paraphasic errors. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Able to recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. Attention intact, able to spell 'world' backward without error. Fund of knowledge appropriate for education level.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally (3 mm to 2 mm). Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally in all divisions. Facial movements symmetric. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally to finger rub. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone throughout. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups of upper and lower extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift. No involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing without dysmetria bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact and symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal gait with appropriate arm swing. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No oral lacerations or tongue bite noted. No skin findings. Patient appears well-nourished and in no acute distress.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of sudden intense fear, depersonalization, and palpitations over the past 2 years, previously diagnosed as panic attacks, now with a witnessed event involving staring, unresponsiveness, and lip-smacking movements
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Tran is a 29-year-old right-handed graduate student who presents to the emergency department after her roommate's partner witnessed an unusual episode at a dinner gathering. The witness reports that during mid-conversation, Ms. Tran suddenly stopped speaking, appeared to stare vacantly ahead, and did not respond to her name being called. She was noted to make repetitive lip-smacking and chewing movements and appeared to pick at the tablecloth with her right hand for approximately 40 to 50 seconds. She then appeared confused and asked where she was, despite being at a familiar friend's apartment. This confusion lasted approximately 5 to 8 minutes before she returned to baseline. She has no recollection of the event itself.

On further questioning, Ms. Tran describes a two-year history of what she and her psychiatrist have labeled panic attacks. These episodes begin with a sudden wave of intense, inexplicable fear accompanied by a sensation of unreality or depersonalization, as though she is watching herself from outside her body. She sometimes notices a brief unpleasant smell like burning rubber that she cannot identify a source for. These feelings are accompanied by palpitations, mild nausea, and a rising sensation in her abdomen. Each episode lasts between 30 seconds and 2 minutes, after which she feels disoriented, has difficulty finding words for several minutes, and experiences profound fatigue. They occur approximately two to four times per month with no clear trigger, though she reports they may be slightly more frequent during periods of sleep deprivation around dissertation deadlines. She has never noticed any motor phenomena during these episodes and has always remained standing or seated without falling. The episodes have persisted despite adequate dosing of sertraline and intermittent lorazepam use, which her psychiatrist has noted is atypical for panic disorder.

Ms. Tran has not previously had an EEG or brain MRI. She was seen in the emergency department 18 months ago during one of these episodes, where an ECG showed sinus tachycardia at 105 bpm and basic labs were unremarkable. She was diagnosed with panic disorder at that time. She denies any history of head trauma, meningitis, encephalitis, febrile seizures, or convulsions. She denies recent illicit drug use, excessive caffeine intake, or alcohol bingeing. She reports poor sleep quality with approximately 5 to 6 hours per night over the past several months.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz, symmetric, well-modulated alpha rhythm reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Brief drowsiness achieved; vertex waves noted. No sustained sleep captured during this 30-minute recording.

findings.location: Bilateral temporal regions, left greater than right
findings.morphology: Arciform, monophasic sharply contoured waves at 6-11 Hz, maximal at T3/T5, without after-going slow wave
findings.clinical_correlation: Wicket spikes are a recognized benign EEG variant and are not indicative of epileptiform activity. However, they can be misinterpreted as temporal sharp waves by less experienced readers.

findings.morphology: Intermittent theta-range slowing at 5-6 Hz, lasting 1-2 seconds
findings.clinical_correlation: Non-specific finding that may be seen with drowsiness. Could also represent subtle focal dysfunction, though clinical significance is uncertain in this context.

artifacts.impact: Moderate, intermittently obscuring temporal regions during periods of tension
Rare, did not significantly impact interpretation

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes of hyperventilation performed. Symmetric bilateral buildup of slow-wave activity. No epileptiform discharges provoked. Patient reported feeling lightheaded and anxious during the procedure.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at multiple flash frequencies. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. Wicket spikes noted as a benign variant. Brief intermittent left temporal theta-range slowing of uncertain clinical significance, possibly drowsiness-related. No definite epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures captured during this 30-minute routine recording.
eeg.limitations: Short recording duration (30 minutes) with limited sleep captured. Moderate muscle artifact intermittently obscuring temporal regions. A normal routine EEG does not exclude epilepsy; if clinical suspicion remains, prolonged video-EEG monitoring is recommended.
recommended_actions: Consider prolonged video-EEG monitoring if clinical suspicion for epilepsy remains
Correlation with clinical history and neuroimaging
Consider repeat EEG with sleep deprivation to increase yield

signal_characteristics.T1: Normal hippocampal volume and internal architecture bilaterally
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: No signal abnormality in the mesial temporal structures

additional_observations: Ventricles and sulci normal in caliber for age
No evidence of mesial temporal sclerosis
No mass lesion, acute infarction, or hemorrhage
Pituitary gland normal in size and signal
mri.impression: Essentially normal MRI of the brain. A solitary 3 mm non-specific punctate white matter lesion in the right frontal periventricular region is of uncertain clinical significance and likely incidental; it is not in a location consistent with the patient's clinical presentation. A 6 mm left choroidal fissure cyst is a normal anatomical variant. No evidence of mesial temporal sclerosis, mass lesion, cortical dysplasia, or other structural epileptogenic substrate.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Early or subtle focal cortical dysplasia not visible on standard 1.5T MRI
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Cannot be excluded - consider 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol if clinical suspicion for epilepsy

recommended_actions: If clinical concern for epilepsy persists, consider 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol (thin coronal cuts through temporal lobes)
Follow-up imaging not required for the incidental white matter lesion unless new neurological symptoms develop

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with no arrhythmia, conduction abnormality, or ischemic changes. No ECG features suggestive of a primary cardiac etiology.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Normal ECG makes cardiac arrhythmia an unlikely explanation for the patient's episodic symptoms.

Additional.clinical_significance: Within normal limits. However, specimen was drawn approximately 3 hours after the witnessed event, well outside the optimal 10-20 minute post-ictal window for detecting epileptic seizure-related prolactin elevation.
Negative urine drug screen excludes substance-induced etiology

labs.interpretation: Complete blood count and basic metabolic panel entirely within normal limits. No metabolic derangement to explain episodic symptoms. Prolactin within normal limits but drawn too late (approximately 3 hours post-event) to be diagnostically useful for distinguishing epileptic from non-epileptic events. TSH normal. Urine drug screen and pregnancy test negative.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz, symmetric, well-modulated alpha rhythm reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes. Adequate NREM and REM sleep obtained over 96-hour recording.

findings.frequency: Rare during wakefulness (2-3 per hour), increasing to 8-10 per hour during NREM sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F7, duration 50-80 ms, followed by after-going slow wave. Dipole field extending to T3 and F3.
findings.state: Predominantly NREM sleep, rare during wakefulness
findings.clinical_correlation: Epileptiform discharges consistent with a left anterior temporal epileptogenic focus. The predominance during sleep explains why a short routine awake EEG failed to capture these discharges.

findings.location: Left anterior temporal onset (F7/T3), evolving regionally
findings.frequency: Three clinical seizures captured over 96 hours
findings.morphology: Seizure onset with left temporal low-amplitude fast activity (beta range) evolving to rhythmic 5-7 Hz theta, then 2-3 Hz rhythmic delta over 45-65 seconds. Seizures remained focal without bilateral spread.
findings.state: Two events during wakefulness (one during reading, one during conversation), one event from sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: Ictal semiology: Event 1 - patient pressed event button reporting sudden intense fear and 'strange smell,' followed by behavioral arrest, staring, lip-smacking automatisms, and right-hand picking movements lasting 50 seconds. Post-ictally confused and dysphasic for 4 minutes. Event 2 - arose from sleep, sat up with fearful expression, lip-smacking and repetitive swallowing, right-hand fumbling with bedsheets, 40 seconds duration, followed by confusion and inability to name objects for 3 minutes. Event 3 - during conversation, sudden arrest, staring, oral automatisms, 35 seconds, post-ictal word-finding difficulty for 2 minutes. All three events had clear left anterior temporal electrographic onset preceding clinical manifestations by 5-8 seconds.

findings.location: Bilateral temporal, left greater than right
findings.morphology: Arciform, monophasic, 6-11 Hz, no after-going slow wave
findings.clinical_correlation: Benign variant, confirmed distinct from the pathological left temporal sharp waves by morphology and context

artifacts.impact: Intermittent, managed with careful montage selection; did not obscure ictal onsets

output.impression: Abnormal 96-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. Three habitual clinical seizures captured with clear left anterior temporal electrographic onset (F7/T3) and semiology consistent with mesial temporal lobe seizures: ictal fear, olfactory aura, behavioral arrest, oral and manual automatisms, and post-ictal dysphasia. The post-ictal language difficulty lateralizes to the language-dominant (likely left) hemisphere. Rare interictal left anterior temporal sharp waves, predominantly activated during sleep, explain why the initial routine EEG was non-diagnostic. This confirms the diagnosis of focal epilepsy with seizures originating from the left temporal lobe.
output.limitations: Muscle artifact during wakefulness intermittently limited temporal chain interpretation, but all three ictal onsets were clearly captured.
recommended_actions: Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy for focal epilepsy
3T MRI with epilepsy protocol to re-evaluate for subtle structural lesion
FDG-PET if surgical evaluation is contemplated

findings.location: Left temporal pole and anterior fusiform gyrus
findings.size: Approximately 12 x 8 mm area of cortical thickening

signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Subtle blurring of the gray-white matter junction in the left temporal pole
signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Mildly hyperintense cortical signal compared to adjacent cortex
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Subtle cortical and subcortical FLAIR hyperintensity with loss of gray-white demarcation, best seen on thin-section coronal images

findings.borders: Ill-defined, characteristic of focal cortical dysplasia
Well-defined, punctate, unchanged from prior study

additional_observations: 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocol including 3D T1 MPRAGE, thin-section coronal T2 and FLAIR perpendicular to hippocampal long axis, and post-gadolinium sequences
The subtle left temporal pole cortical thickening and gray-white junction blurring were not apparent on the prior 1.5T clinical MRI
Left choroidal fissure cyst unchanged, consistent with normal variant
output.impression: 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol reveals a previously occult subtle area of cortical thickening and gray-white junction blurring in the left temporal pole and anterior fusiform gyrus, suspicious for focal cortical dysplasia (likely type IIa based on imaging characteristics). This lesion is concordant with the left temporal electrographic seizure onset zone identified on prolonged video-EEG monitoring. Hippocampal volumes and signal are normal bilaterally; no mesial temporal sclerosis. The right frontal periventricular white matter lesion is unchanged and incidental.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High - gray-white junction blurring and subtle cortical thickening are characteristic
Low - no discrete mass, no enhancement, no cystic component
Low - no history of prior infection or inflammation

recommended_actions: Correlate with video-EEG for concordance (concordant with left temporal onset)
Consider FDG-PET for additional localizing data
Multi-disciplinary epilepsy conference to discuss surgical candidacy
Neuropsychological testing for baseline cognitive assessment

Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: High average overall intellectual functioning, consistent with graduate-level education
Low end of average verbal learning. Given the patient's high intellectual capacity, this relative weakness may represent a subtle lateralizing deficit.

Neuropsychological_Testing.test: Verbal Memory (CVLT-II Long Delay Free Recall)
Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: Below average verbal memory consolidation, discrepant from the patient's overall cognitive profile. Suggests left temporal dysfunction when interpreted in context of high baseline abilities.
Average to high-average visual memory, significantly better than verbal memory performance
Average naming, though below expectation for a graduate student in literature with superior verbal comprehension
Low average semantic fluency, which can reflect left temporal dysfunction

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological profile reveals a notable discrepancy between superior verbal intellectual abilities (VCI 124) and relatively weak verbal memory performance (CVLT-II Long Delay Free Recall T-score 37). While the verbal memory score falls just below the average range in absolute terms, the discrepancy from the patient's high intellectual baseline is clinically significant and suggests material-specific left temporal lobe dysfunction. Visual memory is well-preserved. This pattern, when interpreted in the context of her known left temporal seizure focus, provides additional lateralizing evidence. The relative preservation of visual memory suggests adequate right temporal function, a favorable indicator if surgical resection is considered.
abnormal_values_summary: CVLT-II Long Delay Free Recall T-score 37 - below average verbal memory consolidation, discrepant from high overall cognitive functioning, lateralizing to left temporal dysfunction

findings.location: Left anterior temporal lobe, including temporal pole and anterior fusiform gyrus

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Moderately reduced FDG uptake in the left temporal pole and anterior lateral temporal cortex compared to the right. Mild asymmetric reduction extending to the left anterior mesial temporal structures.
signal_characteristics.asymmetry_index: Left-to-right anterior temporal asymmetry index 0.82 (normal >0.90), indicating significant left anterior temporal hypometabolism

findings.location: Bilateral hippocampal bodies and tails, bilateral frontal and parietal lobes, occipital lobes, basal ganglia, thalami, cerebellum

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Symmetric and normal FDG uptake throughout the remainder of the brain

additional_observations: Interictal FDG-PET acquired during EEG-confirmed interictal state
Hypometabolism is centered on the left temporal pole, concordant with the area of suspected focal cortical dysplasia seen on 3T MRI
No hypermetabolism to suggest ictal activity during acquisition
output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates focal left anterior temporal hypometabolism centered on the temporal pole, concordant with the suspected focal cortical dysplasia identified on 3T epilepsy protocol MRI and the left anterior temporal seizure onset zone on prolonged video-EEG. This multi-modal concordance strongly supports the localization of the epileptogenic zone to the left anterior temporal region.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Focal cortical dysplasia with surrounding hypometabolic zone
differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High - concordant with MRI and EEG findings
Low - patient was in confirmed interictal state during acquisition

recommended_actions: Consider Wada test or fMRI for language lateralization given left-sided lesion in the dominant hemisphere
Discuss surgical options with patient including potential risks to language and memory

Antiepileptic_Drug_Levels.clinical_significance: Patient is not currently taking any antiepileptic medications. Episodes were previously diagnosed as panic disorder and treated with sertraline and as-needed lorazepam. Note: lorazepam, a benzodiazepine, does have antiepileptic properties and its intermittent use may have partially suppressed some seizures, potentially contributing to the incomplete treatment response noted by her psychiatrist.

output.interpretation: No antiepileptic drug levels available as the patient was not prescribed AEDs. She has been managed with sertraline (SSRI) and as-needed lorazepam for a diagnosis of panic disorder. Notably, lorazepam has anticonvulsant properties and its intermittent use may have partially attenuated some of her seizure episodes, which could have contributed to the variable severity of her 'panic attacks' and delayed the correct diagnosis. AED initiation for focal epilepsy is now indicated.

output.query: MRI-negative temporal lobe epilepsy focal cortical dysplasia diagnosis outcomes

results.title: Surgical outcomes in MRI-negative epilepsy: a systematic review and meta-analysis
results.summary: In MRI-negative focal epilepsy, seizure freedom rates following surgery are approximately 40-50%, significantly lower than MRI-positive cases (60-80%). However, identification of a lesion on high-resolution 3T MRI or FDG-PET concordance improves outcomes substantially.

results.title: Focal cortical dysplasia: current imaging and histopathological findings
results.summary: Focal cortical dysplasia type II is characterized by cortical thickening, blurring of the gray-white junction, and often a transmantle sign on MRI. Type IIa may be subtle and missed on standard clinical MRI. 3T or 7T imaging and post-processing techniques improve detection rates.

results.title: Misdiagnosis of epilepsy as panic disorder: clinical features and diagnostic clues
results.source: Devinsky et al., Journal of Neuropsychiatry and Clinical Neurosciences 2008;20(4):446-453
results.summary: Temporal lobe seizures producing ictal fear are frequently misdiagnosed as panic disorder. Key distinguishing features include brief duration (under 2 minutes), stereotyped nature, presence of olfactory or gustatory hallucinations, post-ictal confusion, and lack of response to anxiolytics.

output.summary: MRI-negative temporal lobe epilepsy is an established entity, occurring in approximately 20-30% of temporal lobe epilepsy cases. Focal cortical dysplasia, particularly type IIa, is a common underlying substrate that may not be visible on standard 1.5T MRI. High-resolution 3T MRI with epilepsy-specific protocols and advanced post-processing techniques can reveal previously occult lesions in up to 30% of cases. FDG-PET provides complementary localizing information. Temporal lobe seizures producing ictal fear are commonly misdiagnosed as panic disorder, with diagnostic delays averaging 7 years in some series. Stereotyped episodes lasting under 2 minutes, olfactory or gustatory hallucinations, post-ictal confusion or dysphasia, and failure to respond to standard anxiolytic therapy are important clinical clues.

output.proposed: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily, titrate to 200 mg daily over 8 weeks
interactions: Oral contraceptives (ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel): Estrogen-containing oral contraceptives increase lamotrigine clearance by approximately 50%, resulting in lower serum levels. Dose adjustment may be needed. Lamotrigine levels may fluctuate during the pill-free week, increasing risk of side effects.
Sertraline: No clinically significant pharmacokinetic interaction. Can be co-administered safely.
warnings: Risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis, especially with rapid titration. Must follow slow titration schedule.
Oral contraceptive interaction requires monitoring of lamotrigine levels and possible dose adjustment
Consider switching to a progestin-only contraceptive or non-hormonal method to avoid lamotrigine level fluctuations
Patient has amoxicillin allergy (hives) - no cross-reactivity with lamotrigine, but monitor for rash given drug allergy history
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, titrate to 1000 mg twice daily (no interaction with oral contraceptives, faster titration possible, but higher risk of psychiatric side effects in a patient with anxiety disorder)
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily, titrate to 200 mg twice daily (no interaction with oral contraceptives, generally well-tolerated)
Oxcarbazepine 300 mg twice daily, titrate to 600 mg twice daily (also interacts with oral contraceptives via enzyme induction)

output.proposed: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, titrate to 1000 mg twice daily
interactions: Sertraline: No clinically significant pharmacokinetic interaction.
Oral contraceptives: No interaction. Levetiracetam does not affect oral contraceptive efficacy.
Lorazepam: Additive CNS depressant effects possible. Consider tapering lorazepam as seizure control is achieved.
warnings: Psychiatric adverse effects including irritability, agitation, mood changes, and behavioral disturbance in approximately 10-15% of patients. Important consideration in this patient with pre-existing generalized anxiety disorder and panic disorder (now reclassified as epilepsy).
Depression and suicidal ideation: monitor closely, especially given the diagnostic re-framing that may impact the patient psychologically
Dose adjustment needed in renal impairment (not applicable here with normal creatinine)
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily, titrate over 8 weeks (lower psychiatric side effect risk but interacts with oral contraceptives)
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily, titrate to 200 mg twice daily (favorable side effect profile, no hormonal interactions)
Zonisamide 100 mg daily, titrate to 200-400 mg daily (weight-neutral, once-daily dosing)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (left temporal lobe epilepsy with focal cortical dysplasia, initially MRI-negative and misdiagnosed as panic disorder)

differential.key_features: Sudden fear and depersonalization mimic panic attacks. However, the stereotyped nature, brief duration (<2 minutes), presence of olfactory hallucination, witnessed automatisms with unresponsiveness, post-ictal dysphasia, and failure to respond to SSRI/benzodiazepine therapy distinguish these as temporal lobe seizures.

Pre-existing psychiatric diagnosis of anxiety and the absence of initial EEG abnormalities could suggest PNES. However, the stereotyped semiology, discrete onset and offset, oral automatisms, ictal unresponsiveness, and post-ictal dysphasia are atypical for PNES. Video-EEG capturing events with clear electrographic correlate definitively excludes this.

Depersonalization and derealization are present, but the paroxysmal brevity, associated olfactory hallucinations, motor automatisms during events, and post-ictal confusion are not consistent with a primary dissociative disorder.

Patient is young (29) with no vascular risk factors. Episodes are too stereotyped, too brief, and feature positive symptoms (fear, olfactory hallucination, automatisms) rather than the negative symptoms typical of TIA.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history of episodes focusing on semiology, stereotypy, duration, associated symptoms, and post-ictal features. Interview the witness of the most recent event separately.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Stereotyped episodes with ictal fear, olfactory hallucination, depersonalization, brief duration, witnessed automatisms with unresponsiveness, and post-ictal confusion/dysphasia all suggesting temporal lobe seizures rather than panic attacks
Normal interictal neurological exam, which does not exclude temporal lobe epilepsy
May be normal or show only benign variants (wicket spikes) and non-specific findings in this diagnostic puzzle scenario. A normal routine EEG does not exclude epilepsy.

optimal_actions.action: Recognize that a normal routine EEG does not exclude epilepsy and order prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual events
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left anterior temporal seizure onset with ictal semiology concordant with temporal lobe epilepsy

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to evaluate for structural etiology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Standard MRI may appear normal. Must recognize need for 3T epilepsy protocol to detect subtle focal cortical dysplasia.

optimal_actions.action: Order 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol given MRI-negative epilepsy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle left temporal pole focal cortical dysplasia visible on high-resolution imaging

optimal_actions.action: Obtain basic labs including BMP, CBC, and urine drug screen to exclude metabolic and toxic causes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic panel, normal CBC, negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Initiate antiepileptic drug therapy appropriate for focal epilepsy, considering the patient's medication profile and contraceptive use
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Selection of an AED with consideration of interaction with oral contraceptives and co-existing anxiety. Levetiracetam or lacosamide preferred to avoid OCP interactions, though levetiracetam carries psychiatric side effect risk.

optimal_actions.action: Re-evaluate the psychiatric diagnosis of panic disorder and communicate the revised diagnosis to the patient and her psychiatrist
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Episodes reclassified as temporal lobe seizures with ictal fear. Discuss with psychiatrist regarding potential tapering of sertraline if anxiety resolves with seizure control.

optimal_actions.action: Refer to comprehensive epilepsy center for further evaluation including FDG-PET and neuropsychological testing, and discuss surgical options
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Referral for comprehensive epilepsy evaluation given focal cortical dysplasia

critical_actions: Recognize that the semiology of the episodes (stereotyped fear, olfactory hallucination, brief duration, automatisms, post-ictal dysphasia) is more consistent with temporal lobe seizures than panic attacks
Order prolonged video-EEG monitoring after a normal routine EEG, given high clinical suspicion for epilepsy
Do not dismiss the diagnosis of epilepsy based solely on a normal routine EEG and normal standard MRI
Order 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol for MRI-negative focal epilepsy
Initiate appropriate antiepileptic drug therapy for focal epilepsy
Consider drug interactions with oral contraceptives when selecting an AED
Communicate the revised diagnosis to the patient and coordinate with the treating psychiatrist
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing panic disorder solely based on symptoms of fear and depersonalization without investigating for epilepsy
Accepting a normal routine EEG as definitive evidence against epilepsy when clinical suspicion is high
Dismissing the witnessed event with unresponsiveness and automatisms as a panic attack
Prescribing bupropion which lowers seizure threshold
Starting carbamazepine or phenytoin without recognizing their interaction with oral contraceptives (both reduce OCP efficacy via enzyme induction)
Performing unnecessary lumbar puncture in the absence of signs of infection or inflammation
Attributing all symptoms to anxiety without considering organic neurological causes
key_reasoning_points: Ictal fear arising from the amygdala/mesial temporal structures is one of the most commonly misdiagnosed temporal lobe seizure manifestations, frequently labeled as panic attacks. Key distinguishing features include stereotyped nature, duration under 2 minutes, associated olfactory/gustatory hallucinations, post-ictal confusion or language difficulty, and failure to respond to standard anxiolytic therapy.

A normal routine EEG has a sensitivity of only 29-55% for detecting epileptiform abnormalities in a single recording. Interictal discharges in temporal lobe epilepsy may be rare during wakefulness and activated primarily during sleep, necessitating prolonged monitoring.

Approximately 20-30% of temporal lobe epilepsy cases are MRI-negative on standard clinical imaging. Focal cortical dysplasia, particularly type IIa, can be subtle and detectable only on high-resolution 3T MRI with dedicated epilepsy protocols.

The incidental right frontal periventricular white matter lesion is a red herring that does not correspond to the clinical presentation or electrographic localization. It should not distract from the left temporal focus.

Wicket spikes on the initial EEG are a benign variant that can be misinterpreted as temporal sharp waves, potentially leading to a false-positive EEG interpretation. Correct identification of this benign variant is important.
The patient's intermittent lorazepam use may have partially suppressed some seizures, contributing to variable event severity and complicating the clinical picture.

Post-ictal dysphasia (word-finding difficulty) after the witnessed events lateralizes the seizure focus to the language-dominant hemisphere (left in most right-handed patients), providing an important clinical localizing sign.

AED selection must account for the interaction between enzyme-inducing AEDs and oral contraceptives, as well as the risk of psychiatric side effects in a patient with a pre-existing anxiety disorder diagnosis.

metadata.estimated_complexity: Diagnostic puzzle - episodes misdiagnosed as panic attacks, normal initial EEG and MRI, requires prolonged monitoring and high-resolution imaging to establish diagnosis
key_diagnostic_pitfalls: Ictal fear misdiagnosed as panic attacks
Normal routine EEG interpreted as excluding epilepsy
Normal 1.5T MRI missing subtle focal cortical dysplasia
Wicket spikes potentially confusing EEG interpretation
Incidental white matter lesion serving as red herring
